title: Escalation to Human Review Queue Latency (Art. 14)

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for Art.14.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that EU AI Act Art.14 (Human oversight) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.21.12: Escalation to Human Review Queue Latency (Art. 14).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.21.12' running on index=ai_hil — event=low_confidence | event=human_assigned — decision_id, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=ai_hil sourcetype="ai:human:queue" earliest=-7d (event="low_confidence" OR event="human_assigned")
| eval t=if(event="low_confidence",_time,null())
| eval t2=if(event="human_assigned",_time,null())
| stats min(t) as t_lc, min(t2) as t_ha by decision_id
| eval sla_sec=t_ha-t_lc
| where isnotnull(t_lc) AND (isnull(t_ha) OR sla_sec>900)
| eval breach=if(isnull(t_ha),"unassigned",if(sla_sec>900,"late","ok"))
| table decision_id, t_lc, t_ha, sla_sec, breach
description: Tracks SLA from model_low_confidence event to human_assigned for timely human review per oversight procedures.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for EU AI Act compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ai_hil and sourcetypes ai:human:queue. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Unify timestamps to UTC; (2) set SLA based on risk class; (3) ITSI threshold on median sla_sec; (4) playbook for reassignment. Indexes required: `index=ai_hil`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ai:human:queue`.
visualization: Histogram (sla_sec), Table (breaches), Single value (pct within SLA)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ai_hil` — event=low_confidence | event=human_assigned — decision_id.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Unify timestamps to UTC; (2) set SLA based on risk class; (3) ITSI threshold on median sla_sec; (4) playbook for reassignment.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ai_hil sourcetype="ai:human:queue" earliest=-7d (event="low_confidence" OR event="human_assigned")
| eval t=if(event="low_confidence",_time,null())
| eval t2=if(event="human_assigned",_time,null())
| stats min(t) as t_lc, min(t2) as t_ha by decision_id
| eval sla_sec=t_ha-t_lc
| where isnotnull(t_lc) AND (isnull(t_ha) OR sla_sec>900)
| eval breach=if(isnull(t_ha),"unassigned",if(sla_sec>900,"late","ok"))
| table decision_id, t_lc, t_ha, sla_sec, breach
```

Understanding this SPL

**Escalation to Human Review Queue Latency (Art. 14) (EU AI Act)** — Tracks SLA from model_low_confidence event to human_assigned for timely human review per oversight procedures.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ai_hil` — event=low_confidence | event=human_assigned — decision_id. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ai_hil; **sourcetype**: ai:human:queue. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ai_hil, sourcetype="ai:human:queue", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **t** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **t2** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `stats` aggregates events by **decision_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **sla_sec** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(t_lc) AND (isnull(t_ha) OR sla_sec>900)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **breach** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.

Output fields: breach, decision_id, sla_sec, t, t2, t_ha, t_lc.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Histogram (sla_sec), Table (breaches), Single value (pct within SLA)
grandmaExplanation: We watch for tracks sla from model_low_confidence event to human_assigned for timely human review per oversight procedures. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
